critic_raw:
1. The functional connectivity matrix computation — the primary feature — uses statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting, as explicitly stated. For fMRI functional connectivity, this means the correlation matrices themselves are derived from BOLD timeseries statistics (mean, variance) that include test participants' brain signals. More critically, if dimensionality reduction (PCA or ICA) is applied to the connectivity matrices using full-dataset statistics, the principal components encode the structure of the test-set participants' functional organisation. Since individual FC matrices are high-dimensional (400×400 = 160,000 elements), the PCA components effectively summarise which connectivity patterns discriminate the entire cohort — making it impossible to assess whether the model discovers structure from training data alone.

2. The design uses a "randomized stratified split" but explicitly states it is applied to scans, not participants. With a longitudinal cohort (1,000–1,500 participants contributing ~3.3 scans each on average over 5 years), this means the same participant can appear in both training and test sets. For fMRI-based classification of clinical stage, this violates a fundamental requirement: the model may recognise a participant's brain connectivity signature from their training scans and achieve high test accuracy through identity matching rather than generalisation to new individuals. Participant-level disjoint splits are mandatory.

3. The k-means clustering is described as an unsupervised step, but k (number of clusters) is selected by silhouette score on the validation set. This means the clustering is supervised by the validation label distribution — a form of implicit label leakage. If the optimal k happens to produce clusters that align with the three clinical stages (CN, MCI, ADD), the downstream classifier appears to learn from "unsupervised" features that were already optimised to separate the classes via the validation set.

4. The primary metric is described as "Weighted macro F1" but F1 cannot be simultaneously weighted and macro-averaged without contradiction. Weighted F1 weights each class by its support (reflecting class frequency); macro F1 weights each class equally. These are different metrics with different sensitivities to class imbalance. Choosing between them determines whether the rare AD dementia class gets equal weight or downweighted — which is clinically material. The metric as stated is ambiguous.

5. The comparison between unsupervised fMRI clustering and cognitive tests is structurally unequal in one important respect: cognitive tests measure the same clinical construct that clinicians use to assign the label (cognitive function), while fMRI clustering measures brain connectivity, a distinct modality. If cognitive test scores are used in label definition (as stated: "neuropsychological batteries... for validation"), then the cognitive test baseline has access to a near-label feature, and outperforming it is a substantially higher bar. The experiment needs to specify whether cognitive test scores are included in or excluded from label assignment for the comparison to be interpretable.
all_issues_raised: FC matrix computation uses full-dataset statistics before splitting, embedding test-participant brain signatures into the primary feature
Scan-level stratification allows the same participant to appear in both train and test sets, enabling identity matching rather than generalisation
k-means cluster count selected by validation silhouette score constitutes implicit label-supervised tuning of the 'unsupervised' clustering
Weighted macro F1 is a self-contradictory metric specification — weighted and macro-averaged F1 are different quantities
Cognitive test baseline uses features correlated with label assignment criteria, making it a near-label comparator rather than an independent baseline

Functional connectivity computation, PCA/ICA decomposition, and feature standardization fit on full dataset before splitting, leaking test subject connectivity statistics into the feature space definition

Two-stage pipeline (unsupervised clustering + supervised classifier) is compared to pure supervised baseline; the contribution of the unsupervised clustering step cannot be isolated without an ablation on raw fMRI features
Stratified random split on longitudinal cohort creates participant-level leakage; multiple scans per participant distributed across training and test sets inflate performance through memorization
K-means hyperparameter selection via silhouette on the validation set is circularly evaluated in a feature space defined partly by the validation data itself

Cognitive test scores used as baseline features may overlap with the label derivation process; if MMSE/MoCA informed the clinical stage labels, baseline performance is inflated by partial feature-label identity
Unsupervised clustering dimensionality reduction fit on full dataset before splitting — cluster structure determined using test-set scans, violating the discovery claim
Both models include APOE e4 status; this confounder may dominate classification, and the marginal value of imaging over cognitive tests is not isolated
McNemar test is binary-only; paired t-tests on per-scan scores ignore within-subject correlation from repeated measures — anticonservative p-values
K-means cluster count selected by silhouette score on validation scans that were part of the original clustering — circular validation
Split may not guarantee participant-level separation despite the stated goal — repeated scans from same patients could appear across train and test
